original_abstract: Gifted education in Europe currently presents a landscape of significant contrast. While some nations have implemented sophisticated, specialized programs, others have yet to formally recognize "giftedness" within their legislative or educational frameworks. Despite these discrepancies, many countries bridge the gap through external support organizations. This article, situated within broader holistic research on the subject, examines the diverse pedagogical approaches and organizational structures used to identify and nurture high-potential youth across several European states. A persistent challenge remains the prevailing myth—even among some educators—that gifted programs are inherently elitist or that talented children will succeed without intervention. However, evidence suggests that without targeted attention and early identification, the unique potential of these students often remains unrecognized and underdeveloped. By mapping the systems in countries ranging from the United Kingdom to Slovakia, this study argues for a shift away from seeing giftedness as a guarantee of success toward viewing it as a specific educational need requiring professional expertise.
humanized_abstract: Gifted education in Europe is presented in a highly heterogeneous picture: While some countries have a highly developed, differentiated system for gifted education others have not yet implemented this on a national level through legislation or educational guidelines. To fill this gap many countries have external organizations. As part of a comprehensive, holistic study dealing with pedagogical strategies and structures for identifying and nurturing high ability in Europe, this paper describes different educational approaches and structures for dealing with gifted pupils in various countries such as the United Kingdom, Germany, and Slovakia. The common believe – also shared by many teachers – that educational programmes for the gifted represent elitist concepts and that in any case talented pupils can successfully progress on their own in school, contradicts scientific findings. The inadmissible potential of high ability pupils is only uncovered and developed when they are identified early and appropriately instructed. The study maps these models and structures in European countries such as the United Kingdom, Germany and Slovakia and recommends that giftedness must no longer be considered a guarantee for the success of pupils, but that it should be understood as an educational need, which must be professionally managed.
